---
name: humanize-text
description: Rewrite AI-generated text so it reads like a real person wrote it, without changing the meaning. Use when someone pastes text that sounds like an LLM and wants it cleaned up, or asks to "humanize", "de-AI", "remove the AI slop", "make this sound natural", "this reads like ChatGPT", or similar. Strips the tells (overused phrases, em dashes, robotic structure) while preserving facts, tone, and length.
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, mcp__notion, mcp__google-drive
---

# Humanize Text

Turn AI-generated text into something that reads like a person wrote it. Keep the meaning, the facts, and the author's tone. Strip the patterns that make text read like a language model produced it.

This skill removes AI tells. It does not invent a personality or add opinions. Think of it as cleaning, not rewriting from scratch: the same message with the machine fingerprints gone. If you want copy with a real point of view and voice, that is a different job (write it fresh, or run it past a brand voice guide), and this skill will not manufacture it for you.

## Core idea

AI text reads wrong because it is predictable. Same cadence, same filler, same rhetorical scaffolding, the same three-part lists. Human writing has texture: it varies sentence length, skips the throat-clearing, and trusts the reader. Most of the job is subtraction.

The word and phrase lists below are examples of common tells, not a complete or final rulebook. Models change and so do their habits. If something reads like a machine wrote it but isn't on any list, fix it anyway. Lean on the principles, not just the lists.

## Two modes

Decide which the user wants, and ask if it isn't clear:

- **Light touch (default):** surgical edits. Fix the tells, leave everything else alone. Best when the text is already mostly fine.
- **Full rewrite:** restructure sentences and flow, still preserving meaning and tone. Use when the text is heavily AI-shaped all the way through.

## The tells, and what to do about them

### Em dashes
Models massively overuse them. Default to replacing them with a period, a comma, parentheses, or a restructured sentence. An occasional one in a long piece is fine. Just stop using them as the default connector.

### Overused words and phrases: flag, then keep or cut
Treat the list below as flags, not automatic deletions. For each one, apply a single test: **is this word carrying real meaning here, or is it filler?** Cut the filler. Keep the word when it is doing a real job (a genuinely "robust" load test, literally "navigate to the settings page", an actual "competitive landscape" in a market analysis). Blanket-removing these produces text as stilted as the original.

Common filler flags:

- "real power", "key benefit / insight / takeaway": state the thing directly
- "wake-up call", "game-changer / game-changing": say what actually happened, or why it matters
- "fundamentally changes": say what changed, specifically
- "cut through the noise", "dive deep / deep dive / let's dive in", "unpack": usually just delete, or use the plain verb
- "the good news / bad news", "the reality is", "hard truth / uncomfortable truth": just say the thing
- "it's worth noting / important to note": say it without the preamble
- "at the end of the day": cut
- "in today's [x]", "in an era of", "in a world where": cut the opener, start with the point
- "moreover / furthermore / additionally": "and", "also", or restructure
- "leverage" (meaning use), "utilize": "use"; "facilitate": "help"; "commence": "start"
- "navigate", "landscape", "tapestry" (metaphorical): be literal and specific
- "robust", "seamless / seamlessly", "elevate", "empower": say the concrete thing they gesture at, or cut
- "delve": don't

### Robotic structure
- **Colon-list openers.** "Here's the thing:" or "The bottom line:". Just write the sentence.
- **Tricolons.** "Fast, efficient, and reliable" everywhere. Vary it. Not everything comes in threes.
- **Sycophantic openers.** Drop "Great question!" and "That's a really interesting point."
- **Summary restating.** Don't end by repeating what you just said. Trust the reader to have read it.
- **Mechanical parallelism.** Real writing isn't perfectly balanced. Let one sentence be short. Let the next run a little longer than feels tidy.

## What good output looks like

- **Varied sentence length.** AI hovers at one medium length. Break it: a short punch, then a longer one.
- **Direct.** If something matters, say it. No "it's worth noting that" wrapper.
- **Plain words** over inflated ones, but never at the cost of clarity. Clarity wins every time.
- **The author's tone preserved.** Casual stays casual, technical stays technical, formal stays formal. You are removing machine patterns, not imposing a voice.

## Optional: apply the Capsule brand voice

If a Capsule brand voice guide is available (check the project files, or the brand guidelines in Notion or Google Drive), read it and align word choice and tone to it. If there isn't one, use the rules above. Don't invent a house style that isn't documented.

## Process

1. Read the whole text first. Get the message and the intended tone before touching anything.
2. Pick the mode: light touch or full rewrite.
3. For long text, work section by section so nothing in the middle gets skimmed. Never silently drop a sentence, fact, or section. Everything that goes in comes out.
4. Rewrite. Expect the result to get a little shorter as filler comes out. Do not pad to match the original length.
5. Run the end-pass checklist below before returning anything.

## End-pass checklist

Scan the rewrite for:

- [ ] No em dashes left (or one or two on purpose in a long piece)
- [ ] No phrase from the flag list that's acting as filler
- [ ] No sentence opening with "Moreover / Furthermore / Additionally" or an "In today's..." opener
- [ ] No tricolon that could be broken up
- [ ] No closing paragraph that just restates the piece
- [ ] Sentence lengths actually vary
- [ ] Every fact, number, and claim from the original is still present and unchanged
- [ ] It is not longer than the original

## Boundaries

- Don't change facts, data, numbers, or claims.
- Don't add opinions or information that wasn't there.
- Don't make it longer. Shorter is fine and usually better.
- Don't sacrifice clarity to sound casual. A clear formal sentence beats a chummy vague one.

## Worked example

A faithful de-slop keeps the register. Note that the "after" stays professional. Humanizing is not the same as making it casual.

**Before (AI draft):**

> In today's fast-paced digital landscape, it's worth noting that a robust onboarding flow is a key benefit for any SaaS company. Moreover, by leveraging a seamless, intuitive experience, businesses can fundamentally change how they retain users. At the end of the day, first impressions are a game-changer.

**After (humanized):**

> Onboarding is where most SaaS companies win or lose a new user. A clear first run, where the user does something real in the first few minutes, is one of the biggest levers you have on retention. First impressions decide whether someone comes back.

What changed: cut the "in today's landscape" opener, dropped "it's worth noting", "robust", "key benefit", "leveraging", "seamless", "fundamentally change", "at the end of the day", and "game-changer". Same claim, same professional register, about a third shorter, and the one concrete idea (do something real in the first few minutes) is stated plainly instead of gestured at.

## Maintenance note

This flag list is the canonical home for Capsule's AI-tell list. The client-work-linkedin skill carries a short copy in its "what to avoid" section. If you update the list here, sync that one at release time so the two don't drift.
